title: Shock in the Trauma Patient
learningObjectives: List the four components of the vascular system necessary for normal tissue perfusion.
Describe the symptoms and signs of shock in the order they develop.
Describe the four common clinical shock syndromes.
Explain the pathophysiology of hemorrhagic shock and compare it to mechanical and neurogenic shock.
Describe the management of controllable hemorrhage, uncontrollable hemorrhage, and nonhemorrhagic shock.
Discuss the use of hemostatic agents for uncontrolled extremity hemorrhage.
Discuss current indications for IV fluids in hemorrhagic shock.
Describe when to use tranexamic acid (TXA) in hemorrhage management.

summary.title: What Shock Is
summary.content: Shock is inadequate tissue perfusion—cells do not receive enough oxygen to meet metabolic demand. Normal perfusion requires four components: an intact pump (heart), adequate volume (blood), intact pipes (blood vessels), and adequate oxygen-carrying capacity. Failure of any component produces shock and, untreated, cellular death and organ failure.

summary.title: Progression of Shock
summary.content: Shock develops in a predictable order. Early (compensated) shock shows anxiety/restlessness, tachycardia, cool and pale skin, delayed capillary refill, and a narrowing pulse pressure as the body vasoconstricts to preserve blood pressure. As compensation fails, hypotension, altered mental status, and decompensated shock appear. Falling blood pressure is a late sign—do not wait for it to treat.

summary.title: Four Shock Syndromes
summary.content: Hypovolemic (hemorrhagic) shock is from blood/fluid loss and is the most common in trauma. Cardiogenic (a form of mechanical) shock is pump failure. Mechanical/obstructive shock is from obstruction to filling or output (tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade). Distributive shock includes neurogenic shock (loss of sympathetic tone from spinal cord injury) and septic/anaphylactic causes.

summary.title: Hemorrhagic vs. Neurogenic vs. Mechanical
summary.content: Hemorrhagic shock: tachycardia, cool/pale/clammy skin, narrow pulse pressure. Neurogenic shock: hypotension with a normal or slow heart rate and warm, dry skin below the injury because sympathetic tone is lost. Mechanical (obstructive) shock: hypotension with distended neck veins from a tension pneumothorax or tamponade. Recognizing the pattern guides treatment.

summary.title: Hemorrhage Control and Resuscitation
summary.content: Control external bleeding first: direct pressure, then a tourniquet for uncontrolled extremity hemorrhage, and hemostatic agents/dressings for junctional or deep wounds not controllable by a tourniquet. For internal hemorrhage, the field cannot stop the bleeding—rapid transport to surgery is the priority. IV fluids are given judiciously to support perfusion (permissive/controlled hypotension targeting a palpable radial pulse or systolic ~80–90 mmHg in many protocols), avoiding over-resuscitation that worsens bleeding.

summary.title: TXA and Special Considerations
summary.content: Tranexamic acid (TXA) is an antifibrinolytic that reduces mortality when given early (ideally within 3 hours of injury) to patients with significant hemorrhage or signs of hemorrhagic shock. Keep the patient warm to prevent the lethal triad (hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy). For head-injured patients, maintain blood pressure to preserve cerebral perfusion. For neurogenic shock, fluids plus vasopressor support per protocol may be needed.

clinicalPearls: Falling blood pressure is a LATE sign of shock—treat based on early signs (tachycardia, cool skin, anxiety, narrowing pulse pressure).
Control catastrophic external bleeding first—tourniquets are fast and life-saving for extremity hemorrhage.
Distended neck veins with shock indicate an obstructive cause (tension pneumothorax or tamponade), not hypovolemia.
Keep the patient warm—hypothermia worsens coagulopathy and the lethal triad.
Give TXA early (within ~3 hours) in significant hemorrhage per protocol.
redFlags: Internal hemorrhage cannot be controlled in the field—rapid surgical transport is the life-saving intervention.
Over-aggressive fluid resuscitation can pop clots, dilute clotting factors, and worsen bleeding.
Neurogenic shock (hypotension with bradycardia and warm skin) is a diagnosis of exclusion—assume hemorrhage until proven otherwise.
Do not allow head-injured patients to become hypotensive; hypotension dramatically worsens brain-injury outcomes.
commonMistakes: Waiting for hypotension before recognizing and treating shock.
Attributing all hypotension in a spine-injured patient to neurogenic shock and missing concurrent hemorrhage.
Pouring in large fluid volumes for uncontrolled internal hemorrhage.
Forgetting to keep the patient warm during resuscitation.
memoryAids: Four perfusion components: Pump, Volume, Pipes (vessels), and Oxygen-carrying capacity.
Early shock signs: Anxiety, Tachycardia, cool/pale skin, delayed cap refill, narrowing pulse pressure.
Hemorrhage control ladder: Direct pressure → Tourniquet → Hemostatic dressing.

decisionTrees.title: Approach to the Shock Patient
decisionTrees.content:
1. Control catastrophic external bleeding (pressure → tourniquet → hemostatic).
2. Airway/oxygen; assess perfusion (pulse, skin, mental status, cap refill).
3. Distended neck veins + shock? → obstructive cause (decompress tension pneumothorax / transport for tamponade).
4. Flat neck veins + tachycardia + cool skin → hemorrhagic shock → control bleeding, controlled IV fluids, rapid transport, TXA early.
5. Hypotension + bradycardia + warm dry skin + spinal injury → neurogenic shock (after excluding hemorrhage).
6. Keep warm; maintain BP in head injury; reassess frequently.

references: International Trauma Life Support, 8th ed. Chapter 8: Shock.
Fowler, R.L., P.E. Pepe, et al. Prehospital Management of Shock and Hemorrhage.
CRASH-2 Collaborators. Effects of tranexamic acid on death in trauma patients. Lancet 2010.
